originalTitle: Iraq and Syria sign agreement to restore oil pipeline that would provide alternative to Strait of Hormuz
summary: Iraqi Prime Minister Ali al-Zaidi is visiting the U.S. this week. He met with President Donald Trump at the White House on Tuesday.
content:
Iraq and Syria signed an agreement Friday to rebuild an oil pipeline that would provide an alternative to the Strait of Hormuz.

Baghdad and Damascus signed the deal at a Chamber of Commerce summit in Washington on U.S. investment in Iraq. Energy Secretary Chris Wright presided over the signing by Basra Oil Company CEO Bassem Abdul Karim Nasr and Syrian Petroleum Company CEO Youssef Qablawi.

"There is so much room to drive improvement in Iraq, to raise oil production, to reduce dependencies on hostile neighbors, to bring freedom, prosperity and abundant energy to the nation of Iraq," Wright said before the signing.

Iraqi Prime Minister Ali al-Zaidi is visiting the U.S. this week. He met with President Donald Trump at the White House on Tuesday.

The pipeline stretches from Kirkuk in northern Iraq to Syria's Mediterranean coast with a nameplate capacity of 700,000 barrels per day, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration . It has been closed since it was damaged during the U.S. invasion of Iraq in 2003.

Iraq, the second-largest oil producer in OPEC, has suffered heavily from the disruption to tanker traffic in the Strait of Hormuz during the U.S.-Iran war. Baghdad is reliant on its southern port city of Basra on the Persian Gulf due to limited pipeline options to bring its oil to global markets.

Iraq's oil production fell more than 50% to about 1.9 million barrels per day in June compared with around 4.2 million bpd in February before the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran, according to OPEC data.

Several Gulf states want to expand pipeline capacity to reduce their dependency on Hormuz. The United Arab Emirates is building a second pipeline to the Port of Fujairah on the Gulf of Oman, which would double its export capacity outside the strait.

Saudi Arabia is considering expanding its pipeline to the Red Sea by 2 million bpd, Reuters reported on July 7, citing people close to the matter.

Analysts have cautioned that pipelines can act as a hedge against geopolitical risk in Hormuz, but they do not resolve the underlying threat posed by Iran to energy infrastructure in the region.

"The problem isn't the waterway," Bob McNally, founder of Rapidan Energy, told CNBC's " Power Lunch " on Monday. "It's that Iran can use weapons to attack loading facilities, pumping stations, the end stations, these terminals, and the storage units of these pipelines."
chineseTitle: 伊拉克和叙利亚签署协议恢复石油管道，这将成为霍尔木兹海峡的替代方案
chineseSummary: 伊拉克总理阿里·扎伊迪本周正在访问美国。他周二在白宫会见了唐纳德·特朗普总统。
chineseContent:
伊拉克和叙利亚周五签署了一项重建石油管道的协议，该管道将成为霍尔木兹海峡的替代选择。

巴格达和大马士革在华盛顿举行的关于美国在伊拉克投资的商会峰会上签署了该协议。能源部长克里斯·赖特（Chris Wright）主持了巴士拉石油公司首席执行官巴塞姆·阿卜杜勒·卡里姆·纳斯尔（Bassem Abdul Karim Nasr）和叙利亚石油公司首席执行官优素福·卡布拉维（Youssef Qablawi）的签署。

赖特在签署前表示：“推动伊拉克改善、提高石油产量、减少对敌对邻国的依赖、为伊拉克国家带来自由、繁荣和丰富的能源，还有很大的空间。”

伊拉克总理阿里·扎伊迪本周正在访问美国。他周二在白宫会见了唐纳德·特朗普总统。

据美国能源情报署称，该管道从伊拉克北部的基尔库克延伸至叙利亚地中海沿岸，额定产能为每天 70 万桶。自 2003 年美国入侵伊拉克期间受损以来，它一直关闭。

伊拉克是欧佩克第二大产油国，因美伊战争期间霍尔木兹海峡油轮运输中断而遭受重创。由于将石油输送到全球市场的管道选择有限，巴格达依赖波斯湾的南部港口城市巴士拉。

欧佩克数据显示，伊拉克 6 月份石油产量下降逾 50%，至约 190 万桶/日，而美国和以色列袭击伊朗之前 2 月份的石油产量约为 420 万桶/日。

一些海湾国家希望扩大管道容量，以减少对霍尔木兹的依赖。阿拉伯联合酋长国正在修建第二条通往阿曼湾富查伊拉港的管道，这将使其海峡以外的出口能力增加一倍。

路透社 7 月 7 日援引知情人士的话报道称，沙特阿拉伯正在考虑将其通往红海的管道每天增加 200 万桶。

分析师警告称，管道可以作为霍尔木兹地缘政治风险的对冲工具，但并不能解决伊朗对该地区能源基础设施构成的潜在威胁。

Rapidan Energy 创始人鲍勃·麦克纳利 (Bob McNally) 周一在 CNBC 的“Power Lunch”节目中表示：“问题不在于水路。” “伊朗可以使用武器攻击装载设施、泵站、终点站、这些终端以及这些管道的储存单元。”
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于财经市场信号，适合用于观察利率、汇率、股债商品、金融机构和宏观政策变化。
